text: Laparoscopic right and transverse colectomy with radical mesocolic excision and intracorporeal anastomosis for obstructing transverse colon carcinoma. A seventy-three year old gentleman presents to ED complaining of colicky abdominal pain, nausea and inability to pass stools for several days. Abdominal CT scan shows a subacute large bowel obstruction from a transverse colon mass associated with mild distension of the distal small bowel loops. An urgent colonoscopy confirms the malignant stricture at the level of the mid-transverse colon. The stricture cannot be traversed by the scope and any attempt at stenting fails.

The surgeon performs an emergency laparoscopic operation, establishing pneumoperitoneum and inserting four blunt ports under vision. The transverse colon cancer is clearly identified, with dilation observed in the right colon proximal to the stricture and the transverse and descending colon distal to it. The procedure begins with the identification, clipping, and sectioning of the iliocolic vessels at their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon performs a radical right medial to lateral mesocolic excision, dissecting the duodenum from the mesocolon while preserving the right ureter in the retroperitoneum without breaching Tolt's fascia. The terminal ileal loop is divided using an intracorporeal stapler. The right and middle colic vessels are identified, clipped, and sealed separately at their origin near the top of the pancreas.

In highly selected cases, with an experienced surgeon with advanced laparoscopic colorectal skills, emergency laparoscopic radical resection and primary intracorporeal anastomosis for obstructing colonic cancer is safe, feasible and allows remarkable advantages. in terms of avoiding stoma formation, faster recovery, less pain, less lung infections, and lower rates of incisional hernias and surgical site infections.

The surgeon performs a challenging transverse mesocolic dissection near the duodenum and pancreatic lower edge, carefully following avascular planes to prevent injury to the pancreas. The right colic gutter is mobilized, and the hepatic flexure is taken down, opening the lesser sac by dividing the gastrocolic ligament. The splenic flexure is also completely mobilized to ensure well-perfused colonic tissue.

The surgeon divides the distal mesocolon using a harmonic scalpel and resects the colon at the proximal descending colon with an intracorporeal stapler. The resected specimen is extracted through a six-centimeter mini fan and steel incision using a wound protector. The well-perfused bowel ends are approximated without tension, and a primary intracorporeal anastomosis is performed. The ileal stump is retrieved, correctly oriented, and the bowel stumps are approximated with stay sutures.

The surgeon performs enterotomies on the serosal and muscular layers using a monopolar hook, while the mucosa is opened with a blunt dissector to avoid damage to the opposite bowel side. An intracorporeal side-to-side and isoperistaltic ileodescending colon anastomosis is completed, with the enterotomy closed using a hand-sewn double-layer suture. The assistant elevates the edge of the enterotomy with a stay suture to prevent enteric spillage and enhance visibility of the margins.
The patient is alive with no evidence of disease at one year follow-up.
